title: Memory Fragmentation, Pagination, Virtual Memory and Inter-process Communication
subtopics: Placement algorithms (First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit)

definitions.description: Memory placement algorithm: allocate the first free zone large enough for the new segment.
Memory placement algorithm: allocate the smallest free zone that is large enough.
Memory placement algorithm: allocate the largest free zone (if large enough).
Many small free zones scattered through memory; total free space may suffice but no single contiguous zone is large enough.
A fixed-size unit of virtual address space (typically 4 KB).
A fixed-size unit of physical memory, same size as a page.
A data structure maintained per-process that maps virtual pages to physical page frames.
A small, fast cache inside the processor that stores recently used virtual-to-physical page mappings to speed up address translation.
A technique that uses disk storage to extend apparent RAM capacity; inactive memory pages are stored on disk and loaded on demand.
Pages are only loaded into RAM when they are actually accessed (page fault triggers loading).
A bit in each page table entry indicating whether the page has been modified since it was last loaded from disk; used to avoid unnecessary disk writes on eviction.
A synchronization primitive that controls access to a shared resource; the simplest mutual exclusion mechanism.
The guarantee that at most one process can access a shared resource at any moment.

formulas.name: Number of page table entries (32-bit, 4KB pages)
formulas.expression: 2^32 / 2^12 = 2^20 = 1,048,576 entries  (too large for a single-level table)

algorithms.name: Memory access via pagination
steps: Look up page in page table (check TLB first)
If page not found → page fault trap
Compute physical address = page_frame * page_size + offset

algorithms.name: Virtual memory page fault handling
steps: OS service routine looks for the page in the page file
Load page from page file into an available physical frame

examples.name: Pagination illustration
examples.description: Page size=1000; virtual address 8039 → page 8, frame 3, offset 39, physical=3039.

examples.name: Pagination error
examples.description: Virtual address 5276 → page 5 not in page table → trap generated.

examples.name: 2-level Intel page table
examples.description: Page Directory + Page Table; only tables that are actually used are allocated, reducing memory overhead.

learning_objectives: Compare First Fit, Best Fit, and Worst Fit memory placement algorithms
Explain external fragmentation and memory defragmentation
Describe memory pagination: pages, frames, page tables
Calculate physical addresses from virtual addresses using page tables
Explain the role of the TLB in speeding up address translation
Describe virtual memory and demand paging
Explain the dirty bit and how it reduces unnecessary disk writes
Describe inter-process communication via shared memory
